data.title: Market Close: The Most Important Price Of The Day
data.description:
By Phil Mackintosh, Nasdaq Chief Economist

The official close is undeniably the most important trade of the day.

It determines stock and index returns. It is used for futures and options settlements, mutual fund cash flows and exchange-traded funds (ETF) creations and redemptions.

It is also used to determine the volatility of stocks, the tracking error of index funds and the outperformance (or alpha) of portfolio managers.

In short, it is important.

Close is the largest trade every day

Because the close price is critically important to so many traders, the close is also usually the largest single trade of the day.

As the data below shows, the close usually accounts for more than 10% of the whole day's trading. In some countries, like Japan and developed countries in Europe, it is much more.

Chart 1: The official close is the largest trade of the day

Because of that, investors and issuers care about the close (a lot!). They prefer it to be predictable and stable - reducing, rather than adding, to trading costs. That requires a “good” close to be:

  • Low volatility
  • Low market impact
  • High liquidity
  • Not subject to last-minute shocks or orders
  • Be a price many investors can participate at

Given the close is such a large trade - and such an important trade - the market structure around closes is especially important. It needs to:

  • Attract both buyers and sellers
  • Respond to supply and demand
  • Match numerous different buyers and sellers
  • Produce efficient prices (unbiased estimators)
  • Be simple and understandable to participants

One challenge is balancing the transparency required to attract offsetting liquidity for these very large trades, while limiting the information leakage that might make large investor trades cost more.

As index funds grow, the close is becoming even more important

One important participant in the close are index funds, which are required to run very low tracking error. A liquid and reliable close makes the indexes investable and replicable. Data shows that index funds are growing, which no doubt has been contributing to the growing size of closes worldwide.

However, as we noted here, indexers usually represent just a fraction of close trading. The close is also made up of mutual fund cash flows, options hedging and other trades, which take advantage of a relatively low cost of liquidity (close is usually when spreads and volatility have fallen the most). There are also certain closes around derivative expirations and index rebalances, where the close is even more important, resulting in an even greater percentage of average daily volume.

Chart 2: The official close is the largest trade of the day

There are four main ways to close the market

In trying to optimize the close market structure, there is almost constant evolution and improvement of close processes. But in general, there are four main ways to “close” the stock market. In order of sophistication and complexity:

1. Last Trade

This works just like it sounds. When the market stops trading for the day, whatever the last trade was priced at becomes the “official close.” It's simple.

But this can mean that the close is set by a very small trade, which also makes it almost impossible for indexers to match the close.

2. Weighted-average price (WAP)

An extension on using just the last trade is to use a collection of trades at the end of the day where the price is weighted by the size or volume of each trade, resulting in a weighted-average price (WAP) that better reflects overall market activity during the closing period.

That means the close is set by a larger proportion of liquidity and reduces any “rush of activity” as everyone tries to be the last trade, but it’s still almost impossible for indexers to match the close.

This is mostly used by smaller, less liquid markets - without large index trackers.

3. Auction

Almost all markets start with an auction that computes the equilibrium price where supply and demand clears. Many markets, especially in Europe, also use a discrete (separate) auction to discover close price, too.

This has the benefit of being easy for indexers to match the close - even on very large volumes. However, there can sometimes be dislocations (or price moves) from continuous prices when the auction is unexpectedly imbalanced (has many more buyers or sellers), which can happen on large index trade days.

4. On Close

In North America, a different version of the close auction has evolved. Rather than starting to build the book for an auction after trading stops for the day, it accepts orders (and publishes imbalances) while continuous trading is happening.

That allows market makers to hedge for the auction with continuous volumes. It also tends to make the dislocations (or price moves) from continuous prices to the auction much smaller. However, by definition, more information about the close imbalance is leaked to other traders while tickers are still trading.

Chart 3: Four main ways to “close” the market

Most modern markets use auctions

If we look at closing mechanisms over time, we see that just 30 years ago it was most common to use the last traded price!

A lot has changed in 30 years. Most markets went from open outcry to electronic during the 1990s, the U.S. markets adopted decimal prices, index funds and program trading was created, and volumes exploded.

So, it’s really not surprising that the way markets close has also changed.

In the chart below, we start with data from an academic study. It shows that by 2014, most markets were already using standalone auctions to aggregate large volumes of trades, match them all together and set official closing prices.

We updated this data for a sample of more recent years using a slightly different approach (grey box in the chart below for 2018 onwards, where we also separate WAP and last trades).

We see that the trends of modernization continue. However, with a broader universe, we also see that some countries use weighted-average price auctions. We would highlight that these are used in smaller and less-liquid markets and are also in decline.

Chart 4: Markets have evolved from “last trade” to mostly using auctions for close

Why don’t North American markets rely on auctions alone at the close

One important difference between European and North American markets can be seen in the chart below.

  • When Asian and European markets close, there is other liquidity available in other countries.
  • At the end of North American trading, there is a period where no markets are open, making liquidity harder to source and price.

This makes it much easier to hedge unexpected imbalances that arise in the auctions in Europe and Asia than in the U.S. market.

However, we should also highlight that U.S. stocks and futures markets do still trade after the official close at 4 p.m. Eastern Time.

Chart 5: European markets mostly use auctions; North America uses on-close

The overlap with continuous trading and the close auction book-build is designed to be able to access some of the end-of-day liquidity to hedge the close.

In studies we’ve done, we see that the market reacts immediately to imbalance messages, which the close auction book-build shares. Prices rise (for buy imbalances) and fall (for sell imbalances) - in proportion to the size of the imbalance (line thickness).

Chart 6: MOC volatility in the U.S. is generally pretty low thanks to trading into the close

Some consider this information leakage detrimental to those trying to execute at the close, as it gives other traders a chance to profit from the trades in the close book.

However, as the chart above shows, even for large imbalances, the overall impact or “leakage” from the imbalance announcement is less than 15 basis points. In addition, the difference between the last trade price (red or blue line) and the actual close (parallel grey lines of the same thickness) is, on average, a fraction of that.

In short, the on-close facilities bring additional liquidity into the close - at a pretty cheap price (for liquidity) and a small average profit.

Other ways to reduce volatility

Issuers and investors typically lose when large trades cause prices to move dramatically into the close. There are a couple of interesting ways different markets try to offset that use:

  1. Order types that only offset imbalances - From market orders that only trade on the “opposite” side of the auction book imbalance to limit orders that offset adverse price moves caused by imbalance selling (limit buys) or imbalance buying (limit sells).
  2. Extension period - To focus more attention and liquidity on the stocks that did not originally have an “orderly” close.
  3. Random closes - Make it harder for traders to add or cancel large trades at the very last minute, giving investors and arbitrageurs time to offset excessive market impact.

Chart 7: Some closes use collars to stop prices moving “too far” in the close

Another important feature of the close is the way it aggregates all orders into one book, which helps to minimize impact and ensure all investors get the same price.

If closes were fragmented like live trading often is, there would be multiple clearing prices, depending on which venue orders rested in. This is a problem for investors, as they are the marginal demand and supply.

That means that the market with more buyers clears at a higher price, while the market with more sellers will clear at a lower price. That leaves investors, as a whole, receiving worse prices than they would in a consolidated book.

In continuous markets, these costs are small, as arbitrage can occur between venues.

Auction markets happen instantly. That leaves no time to hedge or send new orders. As a result, market makers run execution and position risks - and liquidity costs would rise.

Chart 8: Multiple closes would most likely hurt investors

As markets change, closes continue to evolve

The trends we see in Chart 4 are far from over. Even markets that have long adopted a specific kind of close regularly reoptimize the rules. For example:

  • In 2016, the Hong Kong exchange moved from a time weighted-average Price (WAP) style close to an auction, in conjunction with a new volatility control mechanism.
  • In 2019, Nasdaq delayed the cutoff to enter orders but also introduced late LOCs (limit orders that could be entered even later) as a way to reduce leakage and still offset unexpected price moves.
  • In 2021, Toronto changed how the Canadian market close works, making it more similar to U.S. models

Close has always been important - to investors, academics and traders.

It's earned a market structure all of its own.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: PagSeguro: Fairly Valued At 9x P/E (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This is my second coverage of PagSeguro Digital Ltd. (PAGS). I first wrote about the company in February 2025 and issued a Strong Buy rating. Since my article, the company has outperformed the market with a 35% return. At that time, the stock was an underappreciated asset that was in a severe downtrend due to a weak Brazilian real (BRL:USD). I believe that now the stock is fairly valued. Perhaps there is still some money left on the table, but I doubt that the stock can beat the market returns from now on. Therefore, I am rating the stock a Hold, as I believe there are better picks in the stock market right now.

In this update, I will talk about the macro situation, their latest quarter, and lastly provide my opinion on the current valuation.

Recent Events and the Macro Environment

In the previous article, I noted that the Selic (Brazilian interest rate) was around 10.5% and used that in my calculation. In the end, interest rates went much higher, and as the company stated, the Selic "grew from an average of around 10.8% per year in 2024 to almost 14.5% per year in 2025."

A 400-basis point jump is a massive headwind for a fintech company that has to fund its credit portfolios and merchant prepayment business. Consequently, as stated in the Q4 earnings call, the financial costs rose 39% year-over-year, directly impacting margins. This macro situation is one reason I am changing my rating on PagSeguro. The second one is valuation.

Q4 Earnings Report

As in my previous article, I will use Brazilian reals to strip out currency volatility so we can better understand the fundamentals.

For FY 2025, net revenues reached BRL 13.4 billion, which represents a 15.5% YoY growth, before slowing down to a 12.4% YoY growth in Q4.

But we have to adjust the growth for inflation, which is currently around 4%. In this case, real growth is approx. 8%. This is much less than I estimated in my previous article, and thus my previously estimated potential upside is incorrect.

Now let’s analyze how revenues translated to the bottom line. Year-over-year earnings before tax expenses in Q4 2025 increased 12.7%, or 8.7% adjusting for inflation. This suggests the EBT margin remains in line.

Credit Portfolio Expansion

The credit portfolio could be the main future driver of revenue and margin growth. It is quite impressive that their core credit portfolio grew 32.8% YoY or 9.9% QoQ, especially considering the 14.5% interest rate environment, that I was talking about earlier. Currently, this high interest rate environment is a strong headwind for the company, and growing financial costs (up 39% YoY) serve as clear evidence. But I firmly believe such high interest rates will not last forever, and at some point, we will see Selic rates decrease. The headwind will then turn into a tailwind, providing a basis for margin expansion. When this happens, I believe, we will again see significant stock price appreciation.

The main problem is that we do not know when rates will be cut, and I am not in the guessing game. As such, this macro environment is one of the reasons for my Hold (Neutral) rating, which will turn bullish as soon as we see rates start to fall. Note that the Selic rate was cut 0.25% in March, but inflation in March also spiked from the lows in February, so I am unsure of further near-term rate reductions, especially due to the crude oil prices being on the rise. Until we see further evidence of lower rates, I remain neutral.

Stock Buybacks

As I anticipated in my last article, 2025 was a strong year for share buybacks. The company stated that ''from January 1, 2025, until December 31, 2025, we repurchased over 27.2 million shares.'' Note that these are impressive numbers, as there are currently 279 million shares outstanding, according to SA. The fact that they bought back approx. 10% of shares outstanding while still managing to grow 15.5% in 2025 is quite impressive, and it gives me a high degree of confidence in the management.

Valuation

PagSeguro is currently growing approx. 12.4% (or approx. 8% inflation adjusted), with EBT growing 12.7%, while trading at a 9x P/E multiple. Non-U.S. stocks are generally discounted, and as such, I believe we are now trading at a fair multiple. I expect that if we do not see any changes in the macro environment, specifically Selic rates, the stock should continue to trade at an 8-12x P/E multiple. But as stated above, once we see further interest rate cuts without the rising inflation, the story will change.

In my opinion, the margins should expand, since the cost of capital will decrease. At this point, EPS growth will reaccelerate, and the stock should continue its upward trajectory. But until then, I do not believe this stock can beat market returns, and an investor should reallocate capital to stocks with more upside potential.

Conclusion and Plan

I believe that currently, it is best to just avoid the stock or have a small tracking position. Should Selic rates meaningfully change, I will provide an update and potentially upgrade my PAGS rating.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SCHO: Still A Bit Wary Of Duration
data.description:
The Schwab Short-Term US Treasury ETF (SCHO) is not that short duration. At 1.9 years it's still a bit sensitive to changes in YTMs around that horizon. Moreover, this exact horizon is where YTMs stand to change the most with the latest developments in the Middle East, specifically the ceasefire on all fronts and the return of parties to the negotiating table. Also, the Strait of Hormuz may see significantly more commercial traffic as of today's peace deal announcement. We think the end of the War hits at the heart of the Fed's rationale lately to keep rates where they are, whose decisions really matter for the SCHO as we covered previously. The end of the supply concerns and the headlines around the war could really affect consumer consciousness and resolve the dangers associated with a re-inflation. Therefore, short to intermediate term rates, around the 2 year mark for example, are the most liable to come down at this point. Given the fault lines exposed by the Iran War, we'd be less confident about betting on capital gains for longer duration as we believe there are still elevated structural inflation risks. The 2-year is a better mark for trying to play FI. But the USD is a risk for us as foreign investors, and the focus may go back to the debasement trade that has been wracking the USD for a year now.

SCHO Breakdown

The expense ratio is 0.03%, which is lower than some similar duration ETFs. The ETF is affected in terms of price effects by changes in benchmark rate assumptions, and very much by the Fed's policy stance in relation to factors currently visible and on the horizon.

Bull Case

The Fed made it clear that with a supply crisis whose scope and duration would be uncertain related to the Iran War and the closing of the Strait, markets might as well forget about cuts. Hence, the market fell, driven by high multiple tech names whose valuations rest substantially in the horizon value, subject most to compounding discounting effects from changes in levels of cost of capital.

SCHO, whose purposes is to be a low risk Treasury ETF, was a little wrong-footed by this and pricing effects therefore brought the investment into relatively poor performance territory YTD.

With ceasefires already here and peace talks underway, and with the Strait of Hormuz opened for the moment to commercial travel, YTM changes are coming in. With inflation risks less severe now, possibly with an end to the main barrier to the Fed's further rate cuts, 2 year Treasury rates are down 7.4 bps as of the time of this writing. A determined end would likely bring that down further as markets should anticipate that a rate cut path can be contemplated again, which is a relative advantage for FI investors looking to stick to Treasuries with the SCHO. The reason why rate cuts were unthinkable with the surge in oil prices is because of how front in mind gas prices are for US households, and how liable higher gas prices are to perpetuate higher inflation expectations that could bake into the economy through wage-price dynamics, perhaps only mitigated by the pretty weak labour market situation with the advent of AI.

Bear Case

The main aspect of the bear case is that for various unpredictable reasons, the conflict may end up reversing some of the benefits beginning to materialise.

But another issue that would be the case even if the War does end is that Treasuries are still USD denominated instruments, but USD-based investments are not necessarily the way to go when FX could become a significant performance detractor again. With yields coming down, the safe haven appeal of the USD falls again, and other negative market narratives could resume. For example we could see a return of the debasement trade. Moreover, the way the war played out may raise questions about US hegemony, where sensitivity to domestic electoral considerations seems to have been a factor for the push to end the War and avoid the economic pressures from higher prices.

Bottom Line

While 10 year rates are for the moment falling more than the 1 year rate, we are uncertain whether that should hold. Higher long term rate assumptions made some sense given the fault lines shown by the war: further fracturing between the US and its European and Gulf allies, apparent sensitivity to domestic electoral concerns, the relative success of the Iranian regime's survival against significant American and Israeli military intervention, Iran's powerful card of being able to interrupt the Strait as leverage point in possible future conflicts. We'd not want to bet on falling long-term YTMs. But at the 2-year mark, we think that a rate reduction path could resume )whether Powell leaves office or not) could be delayed pending Powell's investigation. The Fed independence issue is another reason to worry about long-term US rates, and folds into the debasement trade. The SCHO is lower expense ratio than other similar duration alternatives as well. The biggest concern we have is as foreign investors, we aren't sure we would want more USD exposure than necessary. For that reason we'd rather play the possibility of the end of the war coming with select equities instead.

data.title: IHF: Healthcare Dashboard For April
data.description:
This monthly article series aims at a top-down analysis of the healthcare sector based on value, quality and momentum. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV) and its peers, whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics. This article focuses on a sub-sector fund: iShares U.S. Healthcare Providers ETF (IHF).

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for healthcare providers in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in this industry.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Current data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of writing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by subsector (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

Valuation has significantly deteriorated for healthcare providers. The quality score has improved in pharmaceuticals/biotechnology.

Momentum

The next chart plots median returns by subsector.

Interpretation

In my latest S&P 500 dashboard, I reported that the sector as a whole was undervalued by 16% relative to 11-year averages. The healthcare equipment and healthcare providers sub-sectors are undervalued by 28% and 16%, respectively. Pharmaceuticals/biotechnology and life science tools are close to their historical baseline in valuation, and the former has an excellent quality score.

Focus on IHF

iShares U.S. Healthcare Providers ETF (IHF) was launched on 05/01/2006 and tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Select Health Care Providers Index. IHF has a portfolio of 60 stocks and an expense ratio of 0.38%, while XLV charges 0.08%.

IHF is very concentrated, with a high company-specific risk. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 73.6% of asset value and the heaviest position, UnitedHealth Group, weighs 24.5%. XLV has a similar number of holdings (59) with lower company risk: the top name Eli Lilly (LLY) weighs 13.6%.

In summary, IHF offers capital-weighted exposure to healthcare providers, with high company-specific risk, high volatility and deep drawdowns compared to the sector benchmark. IHF is a risky long-term investment, although healthcare providers are undervalued based on historical averages. This fund is best-suited for swing trading and tactical allocation.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a large healthcare provider company with an Earnings Yield above 0.0446 (or price/earnings below 22.42) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.

It is part of a rotational model with a statistical bias toward excess returns over the long term, not the result of in-depth stock analysis.

data.title: Badger Meter: Finally A Buy Following Q1 Earnings Tumble
data.description:
I've long held the view that Badger Meter (NYSE:BMI) was a great company selling at an unattractive price. Unfortunately, if you significantly overpay for a company, it can take years for the firm's underlying fundamentals to catch up with the share price even if the business keeps performing well.

However, I started to warm up on the firm a bit with my most recent update, upgrading BMI shares from a sell to a hold thanks to a more reasonable valuation in January. After an extended period trading with a sky-high valuation multiple, Badger Meter entered 2026 with a somewhat more grounded valuation point. And things have accelerated on that front over the subsequent three months.

The latest data point came with Badger Meter's soft Q1 earnings, which led to the stock getting clobbered in Friday's trading session. As I write this, shares are off 23% on the day. And in the bigger picture, Badger has given back nearly all its gains from the past five years:

With the stock now down more than 50% from the 52-week highs, enough is enough. Here's why I'm upgrading Badger to a buy.

Q1 Earnings: A Big Miss

On Friday, Badger released its Q1 earnings. Revenues of $202 million fell 9% year-over-year and missed by $29 million. That's a big whiff. Not surprisingly, given the size of the top-line shortfall, the firm's EPS of 93 cents also missed expectations badly, and slumped from the $1.30 reported in the same period of 2025.

The company attributed much of the revenue decline to the roll-off of certain lumpy projects which were completed in 2025 and thus made for a difficult comparative environment.

The miss was primarily driven by a 10% decline in Badger's utility water sales operation. However, flow instrumentation products also fell 4%, raising some concerns about the overall demand landscape as opposed to there simply being issues with utility water sales. The company's gross margin of 41.7%, while still elevated compared to its own historical average, also slipped a bit from the record-high 42.9% which it had reported in the same period of 2025.

One other note from the Q1 earnings release is that Badger announced a new acquisition. It will be acquiring UDlive, a U.K.-based sewer monitoring business, for $100 million. UDlive reported $22 million in revenues and was profitable in its most recent fiscal year. About 4.5x revenues for the acquisitions seems fair, that's not far above where Badger Meter shares have been trading in 2026, and it's well below BMI stock's historical median price-to-sales ratio over the past five years. $22 million of annual revenues isn't a game-changer for Badger, which already does more than $900 million in annual sales. But this sort of bolt-on acquisition, which fits nicely alongside Badger's existing U.S. sewer monitoring operation, should build shareholder value over time.

If these Q1 results are reflective of the future prospects of the business, then there would be no buying opportunity here. This was a really bad quarterly earnings report, there's no mincing words there.

However, management explained that this weakness was largely expected and a matter of timing rather than lack of demand. Here was CEO Kenneth C. Bockhorst's statement:

"Entering 2026, we expected project pacing dynamics to favor the back half of the year [...] Our first quarter results were consistent with that expectation, reflecting comparisons to the prior‑year period when several since completed projects were still in active deployment. Additionally, we experienced weaker than anticipated short-cycle order rates in the first quarter of 2026.

In our view, the year-over-year decline in revenue and associated operating leverage are related to variability in project timing and short-cycle customer order patterns and do not reflect a change in underlying demand, our competitive position, or the long-term market drivers for our business. We have a strong pipeline of awarded projects scheduled to launch in the second half of 2026, and our broader multi-year opportunity funnel remains robust."

Judging by the market reaction, folks seem to be fairly skeptical of this explanation that it's almost all a timing issue and tied to projects from last year which didn't recur.

However, the good news is that Bockhorst stated that Badger anticipates returning to sequential revenue growth next quarter, and that the company is projecting flat overall revenues for 2026 despite the decidedly rough start to the year. What this means is that, as investors, we will be able to confirm or reject management's explanation within the next couple of quarters.

Making The Bull Case

There is now some uncertainty here that has helped trigger the valuation reset. Specifically, Badger Meter was a steady compounding growth story in a high-quality industry.

Water is one of the more basic human needs, and there is little way that it would be disrupted the technological change. And, as people become wealthier, they are willing to spend more to improve the quality of their drinking water. That means plenty of basic water access and solutions investment in emerging markets along with increasing spending for dealing with "forever chemicals", lead, and other water contaminants in developed markets.

Meanwhile, water is also an integral component of innumerable industrial and manufacturing processes. As technology evolves, new highly-intensive water uses invariable emerge. Data centers are the latest such prominent example of that point.

Water also benefits from favorable investment dynamics. There aren't many pureplay water companies listed on U.S. stock exchanges. Most water businesses tend to either be privately-held or operate as subsidiaries within much larger industrial firms or conglomerates. Pureplay firms like Badger are relatively few in number. And there is high demand for water investments as the industry has a favorable long-term growth profile and it also attracts specific capital flows from green- and sustainability-focused funds.

Badger has long attracted investor interest, leading to its historically high valuation multiples. Water meters are a key part of the ecosystem, and smart meters in particular are driving more efficient usage and allowing customers to make more informed decisions. As companies deploy more AI and data analytics into their processes, this should drive further demand for metering upgrades to provide the infrastructure that feeds into these systems.

Badger enjoyed a huge uptick in demand coming out of the pandemic, with the 2021 infrastructure bill providing for a flood of water-focused spending.

I'd argue that the current choppiness around quarterly sales is probably reflective of how Badger Meter greatly benefitted from the macroeconomic landscape over the prior five years. Once a period of above-trend growth ends, investors often start to raise concerns about the core business model and sustainability of the growth strategy. But, in many cases, it can simply be that some reversion to the longer-term mean is in order after a period of unusually robust demand.

It's also worth noting that Badger has significant revenues from its software operations tied to water management. That's traditionally been viewed as a positive, as these high-margin SaaS revenues are more predictable and less cyclical than the water meter sales. That said, we've had a brutal sell-off in SaaS stocks in recent months, and it's possible that the market has discounted Badger's software revenues to some degree as well given current market conditions.

Badger Meter Stock Verdict

With this latest drop, Badger Meter has reached a point where I find the risk/reward outright attractive. After years of liking the company but having distaste for its share price, the stars have finally aligned for investors that want a piece of this story.

I'll grant that 2026 could continue to be choppy, especially if it takes another quarter or two before the anticipated demand recovery starts to take hold. That said, I believe the primary issue here was that the market was too comfortable with Badger given its favorable industry, strong demand profile, and accelerating growth picture. That led folks into complacency and assigning BMI stock as high as a 50x P/E multiple not too long ago.

That froth has now washed out of the market. I don't see the demand for smart meters or water management software going anywhere, though, and I expect buyers to step in as soon as revenues stabilize later this year.

data.title: Public Storage: Why We Took A Large Position In The 6.6% Yielding, A-Rated, Preferreds
data.description:
The Business

Headquartered in Texas, Public Storage (PSA) is in the business of self storage. This REIT owns, develops and operates self storage facilities that lease units on a month-to-month basis. At December 31, 2025, PSA operated 3,533 facilities (spanning 258 million net rentable square feet) across 40 states, with ownership interest in 3,171 of them. This business began operations in 1972 and was serving over 2 million customers by the end of 2025. The credit rating agencies are suitably impressed with this self storage juggernaut and so are we.

The Acquisition

The above numbers provide a snapshot of what was at the end of last year. December 31, 2026 is expected to look quite different, with PSA's acquisition of National Storage Affiliates Trust (NSA). The REIT made its plans public on March 16 and quoting from the press release, NSA "owns more than 1,000 self-storage properties totaling 69M rentable square feet and 550,000 units across 37 U.S. states and Puerto Rico". NSA has around 66% of its properties focused in the Sunbelt states, as defined by the Company (the fine print in the snapshot below notes what NSA considers the Sunbelt).

This PSA-NSA deal is an "All-Stock" transaction, with a 0.14 exchange ratio.

...holders of NSA common shares and operating partnership units will receive 0.14 of a share of PSA common stock or partnership units for each NSA share or unit they own, representing a total consideration of $41.68 per share based on PSA’s closing share price on March 13, 2026.

Source: Press Release

The acquisition also includes the creation of a joint venture with the limited partners of NSA financed with around $2.2B in secured debt, including a $240M mezzanine loan from PSA. As far as assumption of liabilities is concerned, the acquirer will repay NSA's bank debt and senior unsecured notes. Additionally, PSA will assume NSA's mortgage debt, along with Series A/B/A-1 preferred securities. To that end, PSA has obtained financing commitments.

...arranged committed financing of $4.0 billion, to be provided by Goldman Sachs Bank USA and Wells Fargo Bank, National Association, comprised of a $2.0 billion corporate bridge loan and a $2.0 billion joint venture off-balance sheet bridge loan which will become permanent secured mortgage financing.

Source: Press Release

Having received unanimous approval from the board of directors, the deal is expected to close in the third quarter. This is, of course, contingent upon receiving shareholders consent. Upon completion of the transaction, the combined entity will have an enterprise value of close to $77 billion, with close to 4,600 facilities spanning 42 states.

While the common shares of both have been something we could never get behind, primarily on account of valuation, we recently did find one part of the capital structure attractive.

Preferred Securities

PSA has 14 preferred securities, starting from Series F and going all the way down to Series S. A comprehensive list of the alphabet soup has been provided below (prospectus for each can be found here).

As we can see, Series F is second to only Series H in terms of its coupon rate. However, the current yield is what really matters for prospective investors. Recently, we found a few of the 14 quite interesting and divvied up a considerable chunk of our investment dollars between the two.

1) Public Storage PFD SHS F 5.15% (PSA.PR.F)

This Series pays an annual dividend of $1.2875, divided into quarterly instalments of 32.19 cents each. The payments are made on March 31, June 30, September 30 and December 31. They have been redeemable (at $25 plus accrued dividends) since June 2, 2022, but considering the current interest rate, the chance of that are slightly less than zero.

2) Public Storage 4% DEP SHS PFD P (PSA.PR.P)

This Series pays an annual dividend of $1, divided into quarterly instalments of 25 cents each. The payments are made on March 31, June 30, September 30 and December 31. They will be redeemable (at $25 plus accrued dividends) at or after June 16, 2026. If Series F is not being redeemed, Series P, with the lower coupon is definitely not going anywhere, anytime soon.

Moody's gave the preferred securities of PSA a A3 rating back in 2021 and that has not changed.

Moody's Investors Service, ("Moody's") has affirmed the ratings of Public Storage, including its A2 senior unsecured and A3 preferred stock ratings. The rating outlook remains stable.

Source: Yahoo Finance

We picked up PSA.PR.F at $19.65 and PSA.PR.P at $15.20. Both had an yield of 6.6% at the time. The price has increased a bit since then, but not too much. The current yield for both is around 6.30%. We also picked up multiple other series for our clients and in general got about 6.6% yield at the time. The advantage of splitting is that these are thinly traded and it is hard to buy substantial amounts of one, without driving up the price. This was shared within our Marketplace service at the time.

Verdict

PSA pays out $3 in quarterly distributions on its common shares, which based on the current market price of around $302, gives us an yield of close to 4%.

We got over 6.5% for holding the preferred securities of the same A2 rated company, rated just a notch below at A3. Preferred securities carry tons of duration so if we are to compare, we have to look at ultra-long duration bond funds, and of course preferred share funds. Starting off let's look at how that yield stacks up to a fund like iShares 10+ Year Investment Grade Corp Bond ETF (IGLB) that has an yield of 5.5% and holds investment grade corporate bonds. The average rating of the portfolio holdings is lower than the preferred securities of PSA.

You just come out ahead in the game with PSA, and with less risk. We are not even getting into the advantage of dividends relative to interest. Of course there are preferred funds like iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF). Even this yields less than PSA preferreds, while going down significantly in credit quality.

In fact, as we have shown, this fund routinely buys and sells with zero sensitivity to price in the last 30 minutes of the last trading day of the month and creates some serious drag as a result. While the preferred shares are higher, they still remain more appealing than almost all comparable ones. We rate them a buy if you get them at 6.5% or higher yield, but they might still make sense for a switch from some investments you currently hold.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: lululemon: New Styles Are Driving Full-Price Sales
data.description:
In a surprising turn for the markets since the Iran conflict began, the S&P 500 has found footing at fresh new all-time highs. With the velocity of the rebound in the past few weeks, many investors are now asking, are there still buyable pockets left in the market?

I still find vast appeal in rebound plays, particularly in the software and retail stocks that have fallen hard this year. Within retail, I believe lululemon athletica inc. (LULU), the activewear company that has been struggling with maintaining share in the United States, is appealing after a ~20% decline since the start of the year.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on lululemon in January, when the stock was trading just north of $200 per share. While the timing on my buy call was premature, my conviction in the stock remains high, especially after a favorable recent Q4 earnings print that demonstrates the company understands what its issues are and has a plan in place to resolve performance issues. I’m reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

To me, these are the core reasons to be long on lululemon:

  • Reigniting product innovation to return to full-price sales. One of lululemon’s core issues has been stale product, which has led to clearance sales of slow-moving seasonal inventory that damaged not only the company’s gross margins but also the prestige of its brand. The company has released new high-performance training materials, including a new men’s/women’s lineup called Unrestricted Power using new lightweight stretchy materials.
  • Fierce international growth. Even though lululemon’s U.S. sales have been stagnant, the company has been achieving accelerating performance overseas. International is growing at a 20%+ clip, powered by new markets including South Korea.
  • Relatively high gross margins. Though lululemon has been battered by tariffs, the company still maintains a rich mid-50s gross margin profile that enables economies of scale. The potential rollback of tariffs is a further gross margin opportunity for lululemon, as is its improvement in full-price sales.

The core appeal to investing in lululemon now, of course, is valuation. For the current year, FY26 (ending January 2027), the company has guided to 2-4% revenue growth and $12.10-$12.30 in pro forma EPS growth.

And for next year, FY27, Street estimates point to an improvement to 5% revenue growth and $13.38 in EPS, which is 10% growth versus this year’s earnings guidance midpoint. Valuations are further supported by lululemon’s healthy balance sheet, which has $1.81 billion of cash unencumbered by debt (9.5% of the company’s current market value).

At current share prices near $168, this positions lululemon’s valuation multiples at:

  • 13.8x FY26 P/E, or 12.5x ex-cash.
  • 12.6x FY27 P/E, or 11.3x ex-cash.

There’s no doubt that lululemon has a hill to climb ahead of it when it comes to execution. It’s needless to say that reviving brand momentum is tough, especially now in the wake of a weaker consumer spending environment plus the presence of ascendant competition from new brands. That said, risks are priced in with the stock trading at steep discounts to the S&P 500. We also find that lululemon’s discount versus similarly struggling sportswear titan Nike (NKE) is particularly compelling:

Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 Recap

Let’s now go through lululemon’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

lululemon's revenue grew just 1% y/y to $3.64 billion, which still beat Wall Street’s expectations of $3.58 billion (-1% y/y) by a two-point margin. We note, however, that the company faced a tough comp versus a 14-week quarter in the prior-year Q4.

The better measure of the company’s organic growth, as usual, is its comp sales trends, which measure revenue growth from existing store locations, excluding new store openings. Comp sales trends by market are shown below:

There’s good news here on all fronts. Specifically in North America, the company achieved -2% in constant currency comp sales. While there’s a long way to go to full recovery, this is a step change relative to a much deeper -5% comp sales slide in Q3.

Management attributed fresh new products as having a lift on sales during the critical holiday quarter. Also, the company notes that as new styles have made their way into stores, full-price sales in Q1 in the North American market have improved relative to Q4, pointing to recovery in U.S. comps in the year ahead.

Per co-CEO Andre Maestrini’s remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Looking at 2026, a primary goal in the North America is returning the business to healthier levels of full-price sales after seeing a higher markdown penetration in 2025. We are already seeing better full-price sell-through in Q1 relative to Q4, and we are targeting further improvement as we move through the year, driven by increased product newness, innovation and operating discipline […]

And third, we are increasing new style penetration across our assortment. Meghan already spoke to our product creation pillar. So I'll just add that in North America, with our new spring merchandise that is already hitting our stores and website, we have increased our new style penetration to approximately 35%. We know that our guests are looking for new styles and product from us. And when we deliver them effectively, we see strong response. In addition to what Meghan mentioned, other examples of successful new styles include EasyFive and the Groove Wide-Leg.

Sales strength was even more pronounced overseas. Q4 comp sales in international markets skyrocketed 14% y/y, doubling versus a 7% growth pace in Q3. Overseas sales were anchored by mainland China, which grew 21% in constant currency and improved versus 19% growth in Q3.

We still need to keep an eye on lululemon’s margins and profitability. Gross margin of 54.9% declined -550bps y/y, with the company citing a 520bps headwind from tariffs plus 130bps of unfavorably higher markdowns (we’ll need to wait through FY26 to see more favorable full-price sales turning into a tailwind for margins versus FY25’s deep discounting).

Operating margins of 22.3%, meanwhile, fell 670 bps y/y as the company also invested 1 percentage point of revenue more into SG&A expenses on a y/y basis, particularly to build new buzz and brand awareness for the company’s new product lines. Despite this, lululemon’s pro forma EPS of $5.01 still beat Street expectations of $4.78 with 5% upside.

Risks And Key Takeaways

When investing in a turnaround name like lululemon, investors need our “eyes wide open” on the plethora of risks ahead. Many retail management teams have cited a very promotional environment amid a tough macro exiting the holiday season: lululemon may be forced to continue discounting, not just to clear out old stock, but to keep up against peers. We're also disappointed by the company's deleverage on opex costs: in my view, in order for lululemon to really drive a recovery in enthusiasm, it needs to embark on a bigger cost-cutting plan in favor of utilizing AI instead of headcount.

That said, we find risks to be fully priced in at a ~13x forward P/E, on top of a healthy cash reserve. Stay long here.

data.title: GSY: Still Carries FX Risks, Spot Yields Looking To Fall
data.description:
The Invesco Ultra Short Duration ETF (GSY) is an ETF that helps investors back away from any sort of duration conviction, since the index essentially pays out a yield that compensates investors according to spot rate assumptions. While we are still somewhat bearish on duration as in our previous coverage, in this case because the structural and conflict risks remain elevated in our view, short term duration is going to lose some yield on the latest developments in the Middle East, namely all-front ceasefire, continued negotiations, and momentary reopening, without conflict, of the Strait of Hormuz. If this ceasefire endures (which is not a given), we don't have to worry about supply crunch anymore which is going to be important for allowing the Fed to continue with cuts, which they couldn't contemplate while the War went on. But this isn't great for spot yields. Of course, there is no capital gain threat or benefit from changing spot assumptions because there is no duration here. The point is, with GSY effectively equal to cash with a yield in terms of portfolio risk characteristics, we'd move into some select equities, also because GSY isn't going to get a positive capital gains effect from falling YTMs from lower credit spreads and benchmark rate assumptions. Also, for foreign investors, the USD outlook is getting a bit gloomier with maybe the return of trading factors like the debasement trade coming back. Finally, GSY has a higher expense ratio than the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF (FLOT), which accomplishes basically the same thing, though actually better because it truly has negligible duration while GSY has 0.58 years. We'd always pick FLOT over GSY.

GSY Breakdown

Expense ratio is 0.22% relative to the FLOT's 0.15%. Both are basically spot duration bets. Both have mostly single-A rated bonds, lots of commercial papers and things like that - one of the biggest markets for very short term credit, in this case corporate credit. The small credit spreads are also probably falling now on reduced macroeconomic risks related to the War, as speculated for the moment. Of course, the War could reignite and we'd be back to a week ago in terms of market dynamics.

The GSY is attractive when spot rates are remaining high. When they fall, lacking much duration at all at 0.58 years, there is very little effect on price but the incentive to keep cash and the GSY's yield falls. Usually, this will coincide with some sort of positive movement in the market, unless the declines in rates are to tackle a recession.

Bear Case

In this case, the pause of the Iran War and associated risk reduction to shipping through the Strait is a seriously positive thing for the broader market and equities, and warrants moving away from cash, particularly in equities that are lagging the recovery but shouldn't be. The pause the Iran War is already having an effect on 1 year rates, reflecting that the Fed rationale for holding rates and not continuing cuts is becoming moot. The Fed needs to be very cautious when the spectre of reinflation shows itself, particularly as actual inflation risks, and indeed has in fact climbed, over the upper 3% band. Consumer sentiment and awareness of inflation was also at alarming levels that risked wage-price spiral effects through expectations mechanisms. Particularly with the momentary opening of the Strait, where terminals can get filled again, speculation and upward pressure on oil prices can abate a bit. But as such, yield will start trending down. Moreover, there is considerable opportunity cost in being out of the market at the moment, particularly where it is possible to find bets that aren't running up quite as much yet, or were more deeply discounted and still below pre-Iran war levels.

The lessening macro risks is probably also reducing the spot yield from the credit risk to the GSY, but without the duration benefit of lower YTMs from lower benchmark rates and credit spreads to drive a price increase in GSY.

For foreign investors, it's also an opportunity to get away from the USD, which may go back to being a victim of the debasement trade, particularly with some perceiving the US as having projected some amount of weakness in how sensitive the administration was to the electoral risks of the Strait's closure. But more concretely, the USD suffers when the yield curve translates down as the compensation for holding dollar falls and reduces dollars demanded by allocators.

On a specific and comparable note, the higher expense ratio compared to FLOT means there is a strictly better option for addressing a low duration exposure.

Bull Case

On the other hand, having cash in an uncertain moment, even if yields are falling 5 bps or so on an annualised basis only, still affords optionality to respond to possible new dips. The pause in the War could be broken by unexpected military action and the ceasefire may not foster a successful peace deal process. Also, it could take considerable time before logistics sort themselves out. Even the current oil futures curve doesn't see normalisation below $80 per barrel happening until July.

Bottom Line

The ceasefire has deservedly put the possibility of a more sustained peace on the table, with the sides coming together now for a second time after a first attempt fell through. Markets consequently are rallying on the lesser risks and lower cost of capital prospects, which is essential for broader markets with high multiples, large horizon values and related discount factor sensitivities. While it's not about timing markets, there remain picks in more obscure corners that are either still discounted relative to recent history, or are rallying less than they should be. With lower yields coming on cash, and also for foreign investors the FX risk of staying in a USD-denominated instrument with older trading dynamics possibly returning assuming a peace deal is brokered, GSY should be lower priority right now. It won't benefit in terms of capital appreciation either from lower YTMs, both from lessening macro risks for the moment and also from higher probability of rate cuts. Of course, all this is assuming the ceasefire endures into a peace deal, otherwise having cash would be very opportune.

data.title: Telecom Sector May Be Well-Positioned To Handle Inflation And AI Risks
data.description:
Though there are concerns around wireless pricing, Nicholas Clarke, Associate for Investment Grade Credit Research with TD Asset Management, says the telecom sector may be poised to handle risks from inflation and AI. He joins MoneyTalk for a look at the telecom space from a bond perspective.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: The Canadian telecom sector has seen some volatile trading recently amid fears of renewed price war in wireless. But how does the health of these companies look from a bond perspective? Joining us now to discuss is Nick Clarke, Associate for Investment Grade Credit Research with TD Asset Management.

Nick, great to have you here. We talk a lot about the telecom sector from an equity perspective on the show, not often from a bond perspective. How should we be thinking about things?

Nicholas Clarke: Yeah, great to be here. Thanks, Greg, for having me on.

I think that the big thing is you can start with maybe a similarity. And that is investors look for these companies for income exposure for their portfolios. So, on the equity side, that would be a dividend. On the bond side, that would be a coupon or interest payment.

Now, there are differences, of course, in terms of how you get that exposure. And one of the big ones is the risk to that income changing. With fixed income or with bonds, that's a contractual obligation, where dividends are a little bit more earnings-dependent.

In terms of the differences, obviously, when you look at stocks, typically you're thinking price appreciation might be part of the equation here in terms of why I might make an investment. And with bonds, that's normally less so. But we do think in this sector right now, there is potential for bonds to actually increase in value in the next few years. And that's because a lot of these companies have actually taken on a lot of debt, and they're starting to pay it down. And all else being equal, as that debt decreases, the value of the bonds that remain outstanding goes up.

Greg Bonnell: Okay, so nice overview of the telecom space in general. Let's dig into some specific names now. We're going to talk about Rogers. It's been working to bring down its debt levels.

Nicholas Clarke: Yeah, so Rogers increased debt levels earlier in the 2020s in its takeover of Shaw Communications. It's had a little bit longer of a time in terms of getting that debt back down. One of the key strategies that they're working on right now is being able to monetize some of their sports assets. We all know about the Blue Jays-- they own 100% of the Blue Jays. They own 75% now of the Maple Leafs, of TFC, and some real estate assets as well associated with those teams.

What they're trying to do is a two-step process, really, which is to increase the ownership of those teams and those assets to 100%, probably looking at combining them all into one sports entity, and then selling down a minority interest in that to be able to pay down some debt and restore leverage to more sustainable levels.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about Telus. From an equity perspective, people have been, I think, concerned about the stock. I think it's fair to say it's pulled back quite dramatically since the fall. How does it look from a bond perspective?

Nicholas Clarke: Well, that's actually a good context, I think, because with Telus, the dividend yield is quite high. I believe it's close to double what Rogers and Bell is right now. And the question is, how sustainable is that dividend? And they do have a major leadership change coming up at the company. I believe there's a new CEO starting in July of this summer.

So, the question is, is there going to be an opportunity for the company, for management to be able to make a change to dividend policy at that point? And I think a lot of investors still are thinking about roughly a year ago, when Bell cut their dividend in half or maybe even by a little bit more.

So there's definitely some worries about that. But it's a good example of something that would be probably negative for an equity investor but positive for a bond investor in the sense that that would help the company be able to retain more cash flow and use that to reduce leverage.

Greg Bonnell: It will be interesting to see when Victor Dodig takes the big chair, which he-- former CIBC CEO-- what happens with that dividend. But a lot of people are watching that carefully.

Let's talk about those concerns around wireless pricing. I think there were some people on our side of the industry saying, I think the price war has subsided and we can move past this, and now we're afraid of it again.

Nicholas Clarke: Yeah, I think that a lot of people were kind of getting cautiously optimistic on what the sector was looking like going into the holiday season in 2025-- so, think late November, early December. But the first quarter of this year hasn't been quite as positive as people, I think, were hoping, in terms of price rationality or rational price behavior among the big players.

So, what does that mean? Typically, Q1 is a low-load quarter, as they say, which means that there's not as many subscribers being added to these networks as there are in other parts of the year. I believe if you look back 10 years, it's roughly 10% of the annual net adds that come onto these networks, is in the first quarter.

So, we have historical precedent to say that maybe there's not a lot of volume being added at these lower advertised prices. But we're going to have to see as earnings kick off next week, really, what the impact is going to be on ARPU, which is a metric that the industry looks at in terms of the actual price being paid for these new subscribers.

Greg Bonnell: Average revenue per user, right?

Nick Clarke: That's right.

Greg Bonnell: Something I remember from my old days when I used to cover the telecoms a little more carefully.

So, we covered a lot of ground here. With all that in mind, what's the potential opportunity right now for investors?

Nicholas Clarke: So I think there's maybe three things to keep in mind here. Obviously, what's going on in the Middle East is going to be something that is top of mind for most investors. Is this going to cause some kind of energy-related inflation shock? And will that slow down growth? Everyone's trying to figure that out as that situation remains fluid.

So, when we look at these companies, in terms of consumer spending, you think about certain things that families might say-- "Let's hold off on that for a little bit." And I think sitting down with your family at the kitchen table and saying, "Let's take a few months off internet" is probably pretty low on the list. So we look at it from that perspective, in terms of being what we call an inelastic business model in terms of the demand for it. So, we think that that's attractive against this macro backdrop.

And then number two would be, another kind of headline-grabber recently that's been top of mind has been what's going on with AI disruption and private credit specifically. And will that be some kind of contagion that will broaden-out into the market? And we see the sector as pretty safe from that in terms of AI disruption. If anything, we think that it could benefit from it in the longer term. So, we're not too concerned about AI as it pertains to the valuations here.

And then number three would just be, I think, the all-in yields. It's really important to recognize they're still quite high on an absolute basis. And when you compare them to the dividend yields that are being offered, for example, on Rogers or Bell, the bond yields are actually really close to that level right now. And that's not been the case in the past in some years.

So, we'll see what happens with Telus-- if that converges as well the way it has for Bell. But I think those three things are quite attractive. And as we look forward here, we have a number of macro events that I think are going to cloud the outlook for investors in the next little while. And we think there's a safety case to be made, or a defensive case to be made, for exposure to these bonds in this sector.

Original Post

data.title: Carlyle Secured Lending: Portfolio, Yield, And Capital Structure Review
data.description:
Managed by The Carlyle Group, оne of the lаrgest global investment manаgers, Carlyle Secured Lending Inc. (CGBD) has аccess to a brоad nеtwork of dеals and dеep crеdit expеrtise. In this аnаlysis, we exаmine the portfоlio structurе, finаncials, and the rеlative attrаctiveness of the cоmmon stock vеrsus the cоrporate bоnds as of the end of 2025. And if you are interested in our last article for the company, you can read it here.

CGBD In Details

CGBD has 165 holdings. The biggest part of it is in First Lien debt - close to 84%, followed by 6% in Equity Investments, 6% in Investment Funds, and 4% in Second Lien Debt. When we look at the industry exposure, the largest percentage is 18% in "Healthcare and Pharmaceuticals", followed by "Software" at 11%.

The total assets of CGBD as of December 31, 2025, are close to $2.79 billion. Close to 2.7% are in cash and cash equivalents, and around 87% are the total investments at fair value of the total assets:

The total debt is close to $1.54 billion. 100% of the balance sheet leverage of CGBD is with a floating rate. In the next picture, you can see the debt and the debt maturities of the company:

The company's 3-year average annual total return on NAV is close to 9.04%, and the 5-year is around 11.25%. Both numbers are higher than the peer groups.

Historical Premium/Discount

The next chart is a 10-year premium-discount of CGBD. We see its lowest point was during the COVID pandemic. Currently, the discount of the common stock is also higher - close to 28% - and it is at its lowest point.

We see that the discount is more than twice the 5-year average of 12.97%:

Asset Yield, Return On Equity, And Expenses

The annual total investment income is close to $255.6 million. The operating expenses are around $65 million. The operating expenses of CGBD are close to 25.4% of the total investment income. With assets of around $2.79 billion, the gross assets yield is close to 9.16%. The non-leverage gross expense ratio is around 2.32%.

The average loss of yield is close to 0.95%, and for the last 3 years, the average loss/total income is around 9%. When we include all of these, the expected ROE for the common stock is around 6.7%, but because the common stock is at a nearly 28% discount, the discount-adjusted ROE increases to around 9.31%. The number is, as expected, close to the 3-year annualized average total return of the company.

Let's look at the BDC's bonds:

The average yield to maturity of the group is close to 6%. To make the proper comparison with the common stock, we add leverage to the average OTC bond and consider a hypothetical capital structure with 200 total assets and 100 net assets. The expected ROE is close to 8.5%, which is less than one point from the expected ROE of CGBD, and in this case, we will continue to prefer the less risky bond. But this is our opinion - you can always choose from both or none.

Conclusion

CGBD is a well-divеrsified BDC with a high proportion of sеnior sеcured debt and a histоrical performance that outperforms its pеer group. The currеnt discоunt of arоund 28% seеms attrаctive at first glаnce. Hоwever, after adjusting, the expected ROE of the common stock excеeds that of the bоnds by less than one percentage point. Given this difference, the additional risk inherent in equity capital is difficult to justify based solely on the discount.
